stages.expect: A team time trial around Barcelona to open the Tour — the first yellow jersey goes to a rider from the fastest squad. Watch for small but real gaps between the GC contenders' teams, and for the climb to Montjuïc late in the course to shake tired formations.

A punchy day along the Catalan coast with a spiky finale back into Barcelona. One for the puncheurs — expect attacks on the late climbs and a reduced group contesting the finish rather than a full bunch sprint.

The first proper mountain test, climbing out of Catalonia into the Pyrenees with a summit finish at the Les Angles ski station. Unusually early for a GC day — expect the pretenders to be exposed and the first real time gaps of the race.
Classic Ariège breakaway country — rolling, grippy roads through the Pyrenean foothills to Foix. The GC teams will likely let a strong break go and race for the stage from the front group.
A transition day drifting down from the mountains to Pau, the Tour's most-visited stage town after Paris. First genuine chance for the sprinters' teams to control things and set up a bunch finish.
The queen stage of the Pyrenees — over 4,000 m of climbing deep into the high mountains near the Cirque de Gavarnie. A full GC battle is likely; anyone dropped here loses serious time.
Pan-flat through the Landes forest to a big-boulevard finish in Bordeaux. As close to a guaranteed bunch sprint as the Tour offers — the fast men's teams won't let this one escape.

Rolling Dordogne countryside between two beautiful stage towns. On paper a sprint, but the little digs and exposed sections give a strong breakaway or a late attacker half a chance if the sprint teams hesitate.
A leg-sapping day across the Massif Central with barely a metre of flat road — over 3,200 m of climbing without a single famous col. Prime breakaway territory on the eve of the first rest day.

Medium mountains in the Cantal volcanoes with a finish at Le Lioran — steep, irregular climbs that suit ambushes more than set-piece mountain trains. Rest-day legs can be unpredictable; expect aggression.
A gentle run north along the Allier valley. Sprinters' day — short by Tour standards, so a fast, controlled race and a full-gas bunch finish in Nevers is the likely script.

A novelty start on the old French Grand Prix circuit at Magny-Cours, then east across Burgundy. Another one for the sprinters, though open farmland means crosswind echelons could split things if the weather plays along.
The longest stage of the race, grinding along the edge of the Jura to Belfort. Distance plus constant rolling terrain makes this a strongman's breakaway day — expect a big fight to get in the move.

A Vosges classic: short, dense with climbing, finishing up at Le Markstein. The gradients aren't Alpine but the climbs come relentlessly — a day where a GC team can set a trap and an off-day is brutally punished.

Into the Alps at last, capped by the Plateau de Solaison — a savage, little-used summit finish averaging over 9%. With a rest day to follow, riders can empty the tank; expect real GC damage on the final climb.

The race against the clock along the shore of Lake Geneva — 26 km, mostly flat and fast. Pure power-TT terrain: the specialists and strongest GC riders can take a minute or more from the pure climbers here.

Officially flat but with plenty of lumps threading between the Chartreuse massifs. The sprinters' teams will want it, but it's the sort of stage a smart breakaway steals — the last realistic chance before the high Alps.

A long Alpine haul to the ski station at Orcières-Merlette, a climb with Tour history back to Ocaña in 1971. The finale rises steadily rather than savagely — perfect for a sustained team-led GC assault.

Short, sharp and explosive: under 130 km from Gap to the 21 hairpins of Alpe d'Huez. Short mountain stages breed chaos — expect long-range attacks and the GC race blown open on the most famous climb in cycling.

The queen stage: 5,600 m of climbing looping through the Oisans before finishing up Alpe d'Huez a second day running. The last mountain of the Tour and the final chance to overturn the GC — everything gets settled here.

The processional ride into Paris — champagne, photos and truce until the race hits the Champs-Élysées circuit, then the most prestigious sprint of the season. The yellow jersey just has to stay upright.
